plainMeaning: In the second vow I promise not to tell big lies to help others, and I now look back and confess the five ways I may have slipped: a hasty false accusation, exposing someone's secret, betraying my spouse's or friend's confidence, teaching what is false, and forging documents.
recitation: 2. bījuṁ sthūla mṛṣāvāda viramaṇa vrata pañcavidha — kannālīe, govālīe, bhūmālīe, nāsāvahāre, kūḍasakkhijje. e pāñca moṭakāṁ kūḍāṁ āpaṇane kāje, svajanane kāje, dharmane kāje mūkī, parakāje kūḍuṁ bolavā niyama. sūkṣma alikataṇī jayaṇā. e bījā sthūla mṛṣāvāda viramaṇa vrata taṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ. sahassābhakkhāṇo, rahassābhakkhāṇo, sadārāmantabhee, mosovaese, kūḍalehakaraṇe. sahasātkāre — hārate, phīṭate, kuṇaha-pratye kūḍuṁ āḷa dīdhuṁ hoy. e amukātaṇuṁ kāma amukej kīdhuṁ, isyuṁ bhaṇyuṁ hoy. rahassābhakkhāṇo — bejaṇa ekānta mantra mantratā dekhī, meṁ jāṇyuṁ tume, amukuṁ amukuṁ rājaviruddha ālocyo cho, isyuṁ bolyuṁ hoy. svakīya kalatra mitrataṇo mantra anerā āgaḷe prakāśyo hoy. mṛṣā-kūḍā siddhāntataṇo upadeśa parapratye dīdho hoy, athavā anerā pāse kūḍuṁ bolāvyuṁ hoy. kūḍā lekha lakhāvyā hoy, maśībheda kīdho hoy, kūḍī huṇḍī, kūḍī mudrā sañcārī hoy, tathā kuṇahaśuṁ kūḍo jhagaḍo māṇḍyo hoy, huṁ jāṇuṁ chuṁ tujane nidhāna lādhuṁ che, tuja kane amukā amukātaṇuṁ dravya rahyuṁ che, isyuṁ bolyuṁ hoy. aneruṁ e bījā sthūla mṛṣāvāda viramaṇa vrata viṣe.

literalTranslation: Second: the gross vow of abstaining from false speech, fivefold — the falsehood about a girl, the falsehood about cattle, the falsehood about land, the embezzling of a deposit, and false witness. Setting aside these five great lies told for one's own sake, for one's kin's sake, and for religion's sake, the resolve is against speaking a lie for another's worldly sake; for subtle falsehood there is jayaṇā (careful guarding). I now examine the five transgressions of this second vow of abstaining from false speech. Sahasā-abhyākhyāna, rahasya-abhyākhyāna, svadāra-mantra-bheda, mṛṣopadeśa, kūṭalekha-karaṇa: rashly — on losing something, on something going missing — I may have leveled a false charge against someone, saying 'so-and-so's deed was done by such-a-one.' Rahasya-abhyākhyāna: seeing two people conferring in private, I may have said, 'I knew it — you have plotted such-and-such treason against the king'; or I may have disclosed before others the private counsel of my own wife or friend. I may have given to another the teaching of a false doctrine, or made another speak a lie. I may have had forged documents written, doctored a record (ink-tampering), passed off a false bill of exchange or a false seal, or instituted a baseless lawsuit against someone, or said, 'I know a treasure has come to you,' 'such-and-such person's wealth is lodged with you.' Whatever else (occurred) regarding this second gross vow of abstaining from false speech.
idiomaticTranslation: Second comes the householder's vow against gross falsehood, which forbids the five great lies: lying about a person, about cattle, about land, denying a trust placed in one's care, and bearing false witness. Even lies told to protect myself, my family, or my faith are not excused — and my resolve is firm against lying for someone else's worldly advantage; only subtle, unavoidable falsehood is left to careful guarding. Now I search my conduct for the vow's five slips. (1) A rash false accusation: when something was lost or went missing, did I hastily pin the blame on someone — 'that deed was done by so-and-so'? (2) Betraying a secret: seeing two people talking quietly, did I declare, 'I know — you are plotting treason,' or did I expose my own spouse's or friend's confidence to outsiders? (3,4) Did I instruct anyone in false teaching, or put a lie in another's mouth? (5) Did I have false papers drawn up, tamper with a record, circulate a forged bill or seal, start a groundless lawsuit, or threaten someone with 'I know you've found a hoard,' 'so-and-so's money is hidden with you'? For all of this, and any other lapse against this second vow — may it be undone.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having confessed harm to life in the first vow, the practitioner now turns from the hand to the tongue. The verse is built in two tiers that a reader should not confuse. First it fixes the vow's SCOPE through the classical five 'great lies' (moṭā kūḍā): kanyālīka (about people), gavālīka (about animals), bhūmyalīka (about land), nyāsāpahāra (denying a deposit), and kūṭasākṣya (false witness) — the gross falsehoods that wreck reputations, property, and justice. A striking ethical sharpness follows: lies told 'for my own sake, my kin's sake, even for religion's sake' are explicitly set aside (mūkī) — not licensed but excluded from any excuse — and the lay resolve squarely targets lying parakāje, for another's worldly gain, while subtle falsehood is met by jayaṇā. The second tier quotes the five Prakrit atichāra-names and glosses each with vivid, courtroom-and-counting-house detail: the rash blame thrown when something is lost (sahasā-abhyākhyāna); the dangerous 'I know your secret — you are plotting against the king,' and the betrayal of a spouse's or friend's confidence (rahasya-abhyākhyāna, svadāra-mantra-bheda); teaching a false doctrine or making another lie (mṛṣopadeśa); and a whole merchant's catalogue of forgery — false deeds, doctored ledgers (maśībheda), counterfeit huṇḍīs and seals, frivolous lawsuits, and extortionate claims about hidden treasure (kūṭalekha-karaṇa). The movement from scope to slip teaches that the vow is breached not only by the big courtroom lie but by everyday speech that the speaker barely registers as 'lying.'
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain teaching that satya is violated by speech that is asatya OR merely apriya/ahita — untrue, or true but harmful. Note a tradition-specific point: this Śvetāmbara lay-confession lists the second vow's five atichāras as sahasā-abhyākhyāna, rahasya-abhyākhyāna, svadāra-mantra-bheda, mṛṣopadeśa, and kūṭalekha-karaṇa (the Vandittu / Śrāddha-pratikramaṇa set), which differs from the Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.21 list (mithyopadeśa, raho-abhyākhyāna, kūṭalekha-kriyā, nyāsāpahāra, sākāra-mantra-bheda). Tellingly, nyāsāpahāra (nāsāvahāra) appears here not as an atichāra but among the five GROSS lies the vow forbids outright — a coherent rearrangement, not an error. The repeated 'kīdhuṁ–karāvyuṁ / bolyuṁ–bolāvyuṁ' (doing and causing) enforces the karaṇa–kāraṇa principle: instigating or causing a lie binds karma as the act itself does. And the refusal to excuse lies told even 'for dharma' upholds the primacy of truth over expedient piety.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Read as a daily audit, this verse asks unglamorous questions: Did I blurt blame when something went missing? Did I repeat a private confidence, forward a friend's secret, or pass along gossip dressed as 'I just know'? Did I sign or circulate a misleading document, inflate a record, or pressure someone with a half-true threat? Crucially it indicts the lies we tell 'for a good cause' — for the family, the firm, even the temple — and the lies we get others to tell for us. The takeaway: guard not only courtroom oaths but ledgers, messages, and offhand accusations.

notes: (1) The verse contains TWO pentads that must not be conflated: the five 'great lies' defining the vow's scope (kannālīe/govālīe/bhūmālīe/nāsāvahāre/kūḍasakkhijje = kanyā-/go-/bhūmi-alīka, nyāsāpahāra, kūṭasākṣya) and the five atichāras quoted afterward (sahasā-/rahasya-abhyākhyāna, svadāra-mantra-bheda, mṛṣopadeśa, kūṭalekha-karaṇa). (2) This lay list of atichāras matches the Śvetāmbara Vandittu/Śrāddha-pratikramaṇa tradition and differs from Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.21; notably nyāsāpahāra here sits among the gross lies, not the atichāras — a deliberate recension feature, flagged as such, not an error. (3) Old-Gujarati forms (kuṇaha, isyuṁ, aneruṁ, kane, lādhuṁ, phīṭate) are glossed from context and standard medieval-Gujarati usage; mātrās/anusvāras follow the book verbatim per the orthography rule (e.g. અતિયાર, not અતિચાર). (4) The sealing refrain is abbreviated in the printed text ('aneruṁ … viṣe'); the full 'mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' is supplied by the established per-vow pattern (see v.1) and is not repeated here. (5) 'maśībheda' is read as doctoring/altering an inked record (maṣī 'ink' + bheda); an alternative sense 'forging a seal/signature' is plausible and would overlap with the following mudrā clause.
